track_listing.track: Once On The Seas Of Abyssinia
By The Light Of The Magical Moon

transcript.text: On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well hello there, Thomas the Vance here, TV on the radio, and welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
This evening, archive sessions by Tyrannosaurus Rex, recorded in 1969 and the early 70s, featuring of course the late and much lamented Mark Bolan.
We also have a repeat of the first ever session done by Samson.
We've got some classic cuts by Tommy Bolan, Steve Hackett, UFO, and Santana.
And new releases by Simple Minds and Jefferson Starship.
Not a lot of new releases about at this time of the year.
We also have, of course, the answer to the Friday Night Connection we set a fortnight ago.
And a new one.
Tonight we start with ACDC.
The kids are all right.
And the track was Baba O'Reilly.
I played it specially for Derek Dicker, who lives somewhere.
He won't even tell me where, because he scratched out the name of the lighthouse on the front of the car.
I don't know you're out there somewhere, Mr. Dicker.
Also from Sam Walsh of Manchester to his brother Jed,
who's down there in Portsmouth in the Royal Navy.
And I've got a little thing here from Park House,
110 Buxton Road, Mecklesfield in Cheshire.
And it's for Gavin, Leslie and Steve and John and Jim and Dave,
because they wanted to hear the Who.
Before that, you heard High Voltage, ACDC,
from the album If You Want Blood, You've Got It.
That was for Philip Inman and his family,
who live somewhere in the county of Avon.
Also for a load of gentlemen who live in Atlandudno in North Wales.
as Panty Palin, S.A.G.,

from a gentleman called Dan,
in Crewe in Cheshire.

Status quo, the greatest boogie band in the world.
A track from the album Whatever You Want,
the track was called Breaking Away,
and that's specially for Scott Millington
and Hooligan Smith
of Dinnam Hall Ludlow College, because I know they're quo-fans.
Also, David Wicks, a literate quo-fan, he calls himself here from Leeds,
which is more than I sound at the moment,
wanted me to play that track as well, no problem.
Got a little letter here from Peter Sturratt, I think it is,
of 31 Ascot Avenue, Salem, Cheshire, who says,
Dear Tom, what's happened to the Iron Maiden session?
I'll tell you, Peter, it's going to be played on this programme next week.
Let's now dig into the archives, go back to May 1969,
for tyrannosaurus rex many top of the pops fans in europe will have seen or heard our next guests
although this is their very first appearance on our show a duo with a style quite unlike

A sylvedge in the fountain, swept sweet words from I remember childhood
Words of disguise, not long and hard

Thank you for watching.
Mark Bolan and Steve Took, as they were, in May 1969.
Tyrannosaurus Rex.
And the tracks that you heard were Chariot of Silk, Iscariot,
Once on the Seas of Abyssinia and Misty Coasts of Albany.
Loads of people asked to hear T-Rex again on the radio,
and that includes Gary Wilson of Pentalee, I think it is, in Glasgow.
Also, T. Arkell, good name for a racehorse,
of 22 Seacrest Road, New Biggin' by Sea, Northumberland.
And somebody just called Mark.
And Aidan D. Brown of Leamington Spa in Warwickshire.
Cliff McLenahan of Warkington in Kent.
Oh, we heard this afternoon that the official Mark Boland fan club
is going to put out a 12-inch single,
which is going to include one of the tracks that you just heard,
the tracks they're going to include on their 12-incher will be
Life's a gas, once upon the seas of Abyssinia,
blessed wild apple girl, and find a little wood.
If anybody would like to obtain a copy,
write to the address I'll give you in a second.
The price of the record is £1.65,
including postage and packing,
and the address to get it from is this.
The official Mark Boland fan club,
Post Office Box 5,
Trowbridge in Wiltshire.
The official Mark Boland fan club,
Post Office Box 5,
Trowbridge in Wiltshire.
And if you didn't catch the address that time around,
I'll give it to you again later.
Well, it was three years ago this week that he died, unfortunately.
Tommy Bolan had a track from his 76 album.
It was called Private Eyes,
and that's a real classic from that album, called Post Toasty.
A load of people remember him.
We've got cards to prove it in front of us.
Here's one from Barry Alcock.
He wants me to say hello to Colin and Tim and Martin and Dave and Andy
and definitely wanted a Bolan track.
I've got a card from Philip Reynolds,
who wants to be remembered to all his mates
at universities and pollies over the country.
Viz, he says here, Hugh Martin, Gerard Zed, Matt and Jez.
Little card from a gentleman by the name of Colin Hesketh,
also calls himself the Teaser.
He lives in Edenfield, near Bury, in Lanx.
He says, say howdy to Stephen Mark and Joe Soap and Joe Grimmond
and Duke Leopard and Les Howard, Barry Alcock and Ian Steele
and all the other bowlingites around the country.
And finally, a quick mention to Simon Farrant, that is,
with lots and lots of best wishes from his brother,
who is called Mark Farrant.
And the track you heard before that
was something from a really ace album that's out on Arista.
It's called Real to Real Cacophony,
and it's by a band entitled Simple Minds.
The track you heard was Changeling.
And now, here is DC Santana.
Fabulous session, really good sound on that,
and tomorrow they're doing their last gig of a tour around the country.
I'm not absolutely sure where, if I knew I'd tell you,
but I would invite you to catch them if you can.
That's Samson, and a track called Take It Like a Man.
Before that, you heard Carlos Santana
from the double album came out in 77.
It was called Moonflower.
The track was Europa.
And that was specially for Claire,
who's up there in Norwich,
from Charles, who's at work.
And also for everybody in the fifth year
at Aberdeen Grammar School
with very best wishes from David Jack in Aberdeen.
Now, more out of the archives from 69, T-Rex.
T-Rex recorded during 1969.
The tracks he heard were Pavilions of the Sun
and First Heart, Mighty Dawn Dart.
Miss Bowley, he was a really nice fella.
One and a half minutes after 11 o'clock on the Friday Rock Show,
now it's time to reveal who's won the Friday Night Connection,
the one that we set just about a fortnight ago.
In fact, exactly a fortnight ago.
I have to tell you here that the winner was £10 better off,
and he is John Wingfield of No. 32 Norfolk Drive, St. Leonard's-on-Sea
in East Sussex. Well done, John.
Tracks that we played were Born to Run by Bruce Springsteen,
whose name I can never get out right,
Night Moves by Bob Seger,
and Frankenstein by Edgar Winter.
And the connection went like this.
Bruce Springsteen.
Edgar Winter.

you'd hear Summertime in there.
Seasons.
The correct answer.
And John Wingfield, you've got it.
Congratulations, sir.
He's also set a mighty tasty Friday night connection of his own.
You're about to hear it.
Three bits of music, a definite connection.
Listen.
Friday night connection for tonight,
put together by John Wingfield of St. Leonard's-on-Sea
in East Sussex, and that's a great collection of music.
And buried in those three pieces of music,
the titles, the composers, or whatever,
you will find a connection.
If you think you know what it is,
put it down on a postcard in a letter,
anything you like, any form of communication,
and send it to the Friday Night Connection,
the Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1,
London W1A4WW,
and the one that has the correct answer
and the one that we choose will get a £10 record token.
A little query here from Richard Leonard
of No. 5 Manor Court in Bridgend, Mid Glamorgan.
He says, no brief after this week's connection.
Whether I win or not, please say whether.
We have to name the songs as well as the getting the connection.
It might save me a lot of 10p's.
Well, Richard, it's very hard to say.
In actual fact, no, you don't have to name the songs,
you don't have to name the LPs,
all you have to do is get the correct answer.
But obviously, nine times out of ten,
it's probably worthwhile you knowing the album they come from
and also the name of the song.
But the right answer will suffice,
and may I wish you the very best of luck.
Here's something from a new album by Jefferson Starship.
Steve Hackett from Spectral Mornings,

especially for Dick, the number one Genesis,
and Steve Hackett, Freak, up there in Castleton near Sheffield.
Also for a load of people,
they are Chris and Carl and Stephen, Simon and Rick and Andy
at school of me and my family.
I'm referring there to the family of Richard English,
who lives in Westbury-on-Tyne, I think it is.
Also, Mr Gary Cousin of Newton Meehan's Glasgow in Scotland
is a definite Steve Hackett fan.
Before that, you heard something from a new album
by Jefferson Starship, for my money,
just about the best one they've ever put out
in all the years they've been around.
The album's called Freedom at Point Zero.
The track was Awakening,
and you'll find the album on the Grunt label.
And now, two from Samson.
Fabulous session, that was.
Six Foot Under, the name of the track there,
and the other track you heard was Big Brother.
Both of those tracks, incidentally,
will be found on their LP called Survivors,
which is out at the moment on Laser Records.
The name of the band is Samson.
And somebody very kindly via the phone
has told us that Samson are on tomorrow night in Folkestone.
And our new heavy band session next week
is going to be by Iron Maiden.
OK, let's go back to the early 70s
and hear some more delightful stuff from T-Rex.
X, as they were in the early 70s,
and there you heard Hot Love.
It went on to spend six weeks at number one, that record.
And before that, you heard Jewel, before that, Summertime Blues,
and before that, By the Light of the Magical Moon.
Nice to hear that again.
I'd like to dedicate that, particularly that last track, Hot Love,
to Colby Hines and Claire, who've just spent 14 months apart,
and now they're together tonight at the Household Cavalry Hospital.
They're on the staff, not in the hospital, as patients in Windsor in Barks.
Next week, our archive session is going to be by UFO,
which is actually a good name for a band to close on tonight.
Me thinks.

It goes like this.

Tomorrow on Rock on a 4 o'clock,
we're going to be telling you all about our visit

to check out the rock scene in Birmingham and Coventry,
and we're going to meet the Dickies,
something else you might like to know about tomorrow,
6.30 in concert, ACDC.
Our secretary is Susie.
Thank you, Susie.
Our engineer tonight was Charles Tarcharles.
Our producer, as always, is Tony Wilson, who will now speak.
Good night, all.
Thank you very much.
I'll see you tomorrow at 4 o'clock.
Have yourself a very nice rest of the night.
God bless and take care.
And at midnight, we're together on all wavelengths,
And this is Colin Berry in the Midnight Newsroom.
The headlines.
Steel union leaders have called a national strike over pay.
The shell tanker drivers are staying out.
A nephew of the deposed Shah of Iran has been killed by a gunman in Paris.
All 90,000 workers in the steel industry are being called out on strike
from the beginning of next month over the 2% payoff from the British Steel Corporation.
The Iron and Steel Trades Confederation, which has already called the offer derisory,
decided this evening that the strike would go ahead unless more money was forthcoming.
The Union's General Secretary, Bill Sears, said if miners and others
could be offered rises of up to 20%, then so could his members.
It's obvious that the steel worker,
who's working just as hard as ever he did,
in hard, dirty, difficult conditions,
suffering death, injury,
and also the vagaries of health-destroying practices,
he's not prepared under any circumstances
to accept the low offer that has been made.
Bill says the government have published
their controversial bill on trade union reform.
It's based on proposals put forward earlier this year
and sets out new provisions in a number of sensitive areas.
Under the bill, picketing will be restricted
to those directly involved in a dispute
and then only at their place of work.
A closed shop could be introduced
only if 80% of a company's workforce were in favour
and the government would provide money
for unions to hold secret ballots.
As expected, trade union reaction to the bill has been hostile,
The TUC leader, Len Murray, said it was unfair and irrelevant.
It was a triumph of party dogma over common sense.
Shop stewards representing Shell Petrol tanker drivers
have voted to continue their unofficial strike action
over the company's use of outside labour.
As Nick Jones reports, the union men hope support for the strike will spread.
Although the shop stewards say they've no intention at present
of asking the Transport Workers' Union to make this an official strike,
the stewards are hoping there'll now be support for the stoppage
from the tanker drivers at the 11 Shell depots
which have been working normally and continuing to make petrol deliveries.
Because of the dispute, nearly three-quarters of Shell's 4,500 filling stations
have had to close through the lack of petrol.
And in another dispute, after rejecting a 20% pay offer,
2,000 Esso tanker drivers have banned overtime and are working to rule.
This has already cut deliveries of Esso petrol by a third.
The motoring organisations are appealing to motorists not to fill up unnecessarily.
In Paris, a nephew of the deposed Shah of Iran has been shot and killed.
He was Prince Shafia, whose mother is the Shah's twin sister.
